title: Analysis of Cars
text:
Let’s investigate the relationship between fuel efficiency (mpg) and engine displacement (disp) from the mtcars dataset. Those variables have a correlation of r cor(mtcars$mpg, mtcars$disp) |> format(digits=2).


Here is a plot:

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Generate synthetic data
np.random.seed(42)
mpg = np.random.uniform(10, 35, 50)
disp = 400 - (mpg * 8) + np.random.normal(0, 30, 50)  # negative correlation like mtcars

# Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
plt.scatter(mpg, disp, color="dodgerblue", s=40)
plt.xlabel("mpg")
plt.ylabel("disp")
plt.title("MPG vs Displacement (Synthetic)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
This is a walkthrough on leveraging a randomized controlled trial to apply classical frequentist statistics.
For this walkthrough, we will use a website’s newsletter as an example. The landing page includes a section where visitors can sign up to receive the newsletter. The goal is to increase the number of sign-ups by testing two different calls to action (CTAs).

CTA A: “Sign up for our FREE newsletter here!”


CTA B: “If you find this interesting, sign up for our newsletter here!”

To evaluate which CTA performs better, we will run an A/B test. In an A/B test, visitors are randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs. We then compare the sign-up rates between the two groups to determine which message is more effective at driving conversions.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
This is a walkthrough on leveraging a randomized controlled trial to apply classical frequentist statistics.
For this walkthrough, we will use a website’s newsletter as an example. The landing page includes a section where visitors can sign up to receive the newsletter. The goal is to increase the number of sign-ups by testing two different calls to action (CTAs).

CTA A: “Sign up for our FREE newsletter here!”


CTA B: “If you find this interesting, sign up for our newsletter here!”

To evaluate which CTA performs better, we will run an A/B test. In an A/B test, visitors are randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs. We then compare the sign-up rates between the two groups to determine which message is more effective at driving conversions.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
In this section, we will connect the A/B test to a more formal statistical framework.
Each visitor either signs up or does not, so we will model each outcome as a draw from a Bernoulli distribution with parameter:

\(\boldsymbol{\pi}\) - probability of signing up.

Because the CTA a visitor sees may influence their decision, we allow this probability to differ between groups:

\(\boldsymbol{\pi_A}\) Visitors who see CTA A sign-up probability.


\(\boldsymbol{\pi_B}\) Visitors who see CTA B sign-up probability. In either case, each outcome is binary: sign up (1) or not (0).

The main quantity we care about is the difference between the two sign-up probabilities:

\(\boldsymbol{\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B}\)

This captures how much better one CTA performs relative to the other.
To estimate this, we use the difference in sample means:

\(\boldsymbol{\hat{\theta}} = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\)

Since each observation is either 0 or 1, the sample mean is the proportion of visitors who signed up.

\(\boldsymbol{\bar{X}_A} = \hat{\pi}_A\) and \(\boldsymbol{\bar{X}_B} = \hat{\pi}_B\)

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Simulating Data
In the real world, we would not know the true values of \(\pi_A\) and \(\pi_B\) ahead of time; we would only estimate them after running an experiment. For this exercise, we will set these values ourselves so we can study how our estimator behaves when we know the truth.
Suppose \(\pi_A = 0.22\) and \(\pi_B = 0.18\), so the true difference is \(\theta = 0.04\).

import numpy as np
import polars as pl


# Set seed for reproducibility
np.random.seed(42)

# True probabilities
pi_A = 0.22
pi_B = 0.18

# Number of samples per group
n = 1000

# Simulate Bernoulli draws
A = np.random.binomial(1, pi_A, n)
B = np.random.binomial(1, pi_B, n)

# Create Polars DataFrame
data = pl.DataFrame({
    "group": ["A"] * n + ["B"] * n,
    "outcome": np.concatenate([A, B])
})

data.group_by("group").agg(
    pl.col("outcome").mean().alias("pi_hat"),
    pl.col("outcome").sum().alias("sign_ups"),
    pl.len().alias("n")
)


shape: (2, 4)



group
pi_hat
sign_ups
n


str
f64
i64
u32




"B"
0.178
178
1000


"A"
0.216
216
1000

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers
Samples are a strong way to represent the population.






Important



The Law of Large Numbers (LLN) tells us that as the sample size increases, the sample mean gets closer to the true population mean.


Because of LLN, we can be confident that our estimator \(\hat{\theta} = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\) will be close to the true \(\theta\) as long as our sample is large enough.

# Set seed
np.random.seed(42) # 42 - also the answer to the Ultimate Question of Life, the Universe, and Everything

# True probabilities
pi_A = 0.22
pi_B = 0.18
theta = pi_A - pi_B

# Different sample sizes
sample_sizes = (
    list(range(1, 30, 2)) +
    list(range(30, 200, 10)) +
    [500, 1000, 2000, 3000]
)

results = []

for n in sample_sizes:
    # Simulate draws
    A = np.random.binomial(1, pi_A, n)
    B = np.random.binomial(1, pi_B, n)

    # Element-wise differences
    diff = A - B

    # Estimate (mean of differences)
    theta_hat = diff.mean()

    results.append({
        "n": n,
        "theta_hat": theta_hat
    })



Show plotting code
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Convert to Polars DataFrame
df = pl.DataFrame(results)

# Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
plt.plot(df["n"], df["theta_hat"], marker="o")
plt.axhline(theta, linestyle="--", label="True difference (θ = 0.04)")

plt.xlabel("Sample size (n)")
plt.ylabel("Estimated difference (theta_hat)")
plt.title("Law of Large Numbers: Estimates Improve with Sample Size")
plt.xscale("log")
plt.legend()

plt.show()















Note



This plot shows that when the sample size is small, the estimated difference is pretty noisy and can move around a lot just due to random variation. As more observations are included, the estimate starts to stabilize and get closer to the true difference of 0.04 (since \(\pi_A = 0.22\) and \(\pi_B = 0.18\), so \(0.22 - 0.18\)).

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors
Now that we have some confidence in our estimator, the next question is how precise it actually is. Instead of only reporting a single estimate of \(\theta\), we also want to include a confidence interval to show the uncertainty around that estimate.
Bootstrapping is a way to understand how much our estimate might vary without needing a complicated formula. Instead of relying on theory, we use the data we already have and repeatedly resample from it (with replacement) to create many new “fake” datasets. For each of these, we calculate the statistic we care about. By looking at how those values change across resamples, we can get a sense of how much variability there is and use that to estimate the standard error.






Tip



Fun fact: The bootstrap method was introduced by statistician Bradley Efron in 1979 at Stanford University. The name comes from the phrase “pulling yourself up by your bootstraps,” since the method uses the data itself to learn about its own variability—no external assumptions needed.


First, we take one bootstrap resample from each group. This means we sample 1,000 observations with replacement from the CTA A group and 1,000 observations with replacement from the CTA B group. Using those resampled datasets, we compute one bootstrapped estimate of the difference in sign-up rates, \(\hat{\theta}^*\).

def bootstrap_theta(data, n=1000):
    A_obs = data.filter(pl.col("group") == "A")["outcome"].to_numpy()
    B_obs = data.filter(pl.col("group") == "B")["outcome"].to_numpy()

    A_star = np.random.choice(A_obs, size=n, replace=True)
    B_star = np.random.choice(B_obs, size=n, replace=True)

    return A_star.mean() - B_star.mean()



Example call
theta_hat_star = bootstrap_theta(data)
theta_hat_star


np.float64(0.032)


Next, we repeat the resampling process 1,000 times. Each time, we call our bootstrap function to generate a new estimate of \(\hat{\theta}^*\). This gives us a distribution of bootstrapped estimates.

n_boot = 1_000

boot_theta = np.array([bootstrap_theta(data) for _ in range(n_boot)])

boot_theta[:10] # preview of first 10

array([0.041, 0.058, 0.039, 0.043, 0.062, 0.038, 0.042, 0.039, 0.026,
       0.012])


Once we have the 1,000 bootstrapped estimates, we can measure how much they vary. The standard deviation of these values is the bootstrapped standard error, which tells us how much uncertainty there is in our estimator

boot_se = boot_theta.std(ddof=1)
boot_se

np.float64(0.01770777242354905)


Finally, we compare the bootstrapped standard error to the analytical standard error. The analytical version comes from the standard formula for the difference in means of two independent Bernoulli samples.
\[
SE(\hat\theta) = \sqrt{\frac{\hat\pi_A(1 - \hat\pi_A)}{n_A} + \frac{\hat\pi_B(1 - \hat\pi_B)}{n_B}}
\]
where \(\hat\pi_A\) and \(\hat\pi_B\) are the sample proportions and \(n_A\) and \(n_B\) are the sample sizes.

A_obs = data.filter(pl.col("group") == "A")["outcome"].to_numpy()
B_obs = data.filter(pl.col("group") == "B")["outcome"].to_numpy()

n = len(A_obs)

pi_hat_A = A_obs.mean()
pi_hat_B = B_obs.mean()

analytic_se = np.sqrt(
    (pi_hat_A * (1 - pi_hat_A) / n) +
    (pi_hat_B * (1 - pi_hat_B) / n)
)

comparison = pl.DataFrame({
    "method": ["Bootstrap SE", "Analytical SE"],
    "value": [boot_se, analytic_se]
})

comparison


shape: (2, 2)



method
value


str
f64




"Bootstrap SE"
0.017708


"Analytical SE"
0.017767












Note



The bootstrapped standard error and analytical standard error are extremely close to each other, with only a very small difference between them. This suggests that the bootstrap method is doing a good job estimating the variability of our estimator, even without relying on the analytical formula.



Confidence Interval
To construct a 95% confidence interval, we use the fact that our estimator is approximately normally distributed (we will explain why this is a reasonable assumption using the Central Limit Theorem in the next section). The value 1.96 comes from the standard normal distribution and represents the cutoff needed to capture the middle 95% of possible values. Using this, we take our estimate of \(\theta\) and add and subtract \(1.96 \times\) the standard error to get a range of plausible values for the true difference.

theta_hat = (
    data.filter(pl.col("group") == "A")["outcome"].mean()
    - data.filter(pl.col("group") == "B")["outcome"].mean()
)

ci_lower = theta_hat - 1.96 * boot_se
ci_upper = theta_hat + 1.96 * boot_se

(ci_lower, ci_upper)

(np.float64(0.0032927660498438677), np.float64(0.07270723395015614))




Show plotting code
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Plot bootstrap distribution
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
plt.hist(boot_theta, bins=30)

# Add vertical lines
plt.axvline(theta_hat, linestyle="-", linewidth=2, color="black", label="Estimate (θ̂)")
plt.axvline(ci_lower, linestyle="--", linewidth=2, color="red", label="Lower 95% CI")
plt.axvline(ci_upper, linestyle=":", linewidth=2, color="blue", label="Upper 95% CI")

plt.xlabel("Bootstrapped estimates of theta")
plt.ylabel("Frequency")
plt.title("Bootstrap Distribution with 95% Confidence Interval")
plt.legend()

plt.show()

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Central Limit Theorem






Important



The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) tells us that as our sample size gets larger, the distribution of our estimator starts to look approximately normal, even if the original data is not normally distributed.


In other words, regardless of the shape of the underlying population, the sample mean (and therefore the difference in sample means) will tend to follow a normal distribution when we have enough observations.

Then demonstrate this visually by creating four histograms, one for each of the following sample sizes: \(n \in \{25, 50, 100, 500\}\).
For each sample size \(n\):

Draw \(n\) observations from the CTA A distribution and \(n\) observations from the CTA B distribution.
Compute \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\).
Repeat steps 1–2 a total of 1,000 times to produce 1,000 values of \(\hat\theta\).
Plot a histogram of those 1,000 values.

Arrange the four histograms so they can be compared easily (e.g., in a 2x2 grid with consistent x-axis scales).



Show histogram code
def simulate_theta(pi_A, pi_B, n):
    A = np.random.binomial(1, pi_A, n)
    B = np.random.binomial(1, pi_B, n)

    return A.mean() - B.mean()

# Sample sizes to compare
sample_sizes = [25, 50, 100, 500]

# Number of repeated simulations
n_sim = 1000

# Store results
theta_results = {}

for n in sample_sizes:
    theta_hats = np.array([
        simulate_theta(pi_A, pi_B, n) for _ in range(n_sim)
    ])

    theta_results[n] = theta_hats




Show plotting code
all_theta = np.concatenate(list(theta_results.values()))
x_min = all_theta.min()
x_max = all_theta.max()

# Create 2x2 grid of histograms
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 8), sharex=True, sharey=True)
axes = axes.flatten()

for ax, n in zip(axes, sample_sizes):
    ax.hist(theta_results[n], bins=30, edgecolor="black")
    ax.axvline(0.04, linestyle="--", color="red", linewidth=2, label="True difference = 0.04")
    ax.set_title(f"n = {n}")
    ax.set_xlabel("Estimated difference (theta_hat)")
    ax.set_ylabel("Frequency")
    ax.set_xlim(x_min, x_max)

# Add one legend for the full figure
handles, labels = axes[0].get_legend_handles_labels()
fig.legend(handles, labels, loc="upper right")

plt.suptitle("Sampling Distribution of theta_hat at Different Sample Sizes", y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









When the sample size is small, the distribution of \(\hat{\theta}\) looks pretty rough and can be a bit uneven due to randomness. As we increase the sample size, the distribution starts to smooth out and becomes more centered around the true value of 0.04. Eventually, it begins to take on a clear bell-shaped form. This is the Central Limit Theorem in action—larger samples lead to a more normal-looking distribution.

Move the slider below to change the sample size and see how the distribution of \(\hat{\theta}\) evolves as we include more observations.



Show slider code
import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go

# Set seed for reproducibility
np.random.seed(42)

# True probabilities
pi_A = 0.22
pi_B = 0.18
true_theta = pi_A - pi_B

# Sample sizes the reader can explore
sample_sizes = [1, 5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 250, 500, 1000]

# Number of simulated estimates per sample size
n_sim = 1000

# Precompute sampling distributions
theta_results = {}

for n in sample_sizes:
    theta_hats = []
    for _ in range(n_sim):
        A = np.random.binomial(1, pi_A, n)
        B = np.random.binomial(1, pi_B, n)
        theta_hats.append(A.mean() - B.mean())
    theta_results[n] = np.array(theta_hats)

# Common x-range for consistency across frames
all_theta = np.concatenate(list(theta_results.values()))
x_min = all_theta.min()
x_max = all_theta.max()

# Add a little padding so endpoint values like +1 are not lost on the boundary
pad = 0.05
bin_start = x_min - pad
bin_end = x_max + pad
bin_size = (bin_end - bin_start) / 30

# Build frames
frames = []
for n in sample_sizes:
    hist = go.Histogram(
        x=theta_results[n],
        nbinsx=30,
        name=f"n = {n}",
        xbins=dict(start=bin_start, end=bin_end, size=bin_size)
    )

    frames.append(
        go.Frame(
            data=[hist],
            name=str(n),
            layout=go.Layout(
                title_text=f"Sampling Distribution of theta_hat (n = {n})"
            )
        )
    )

# Initial histogram
initial_n = sample_sizes[0]

fig = go.Figure(
    data=[
        go.Histogram(
            x=theta_results[initial_n],
            nbinsx=30,
            xbins=dict(start=bin_start, end=bin_end, size=bin_size)
        )
    ],
    layout=go.Layout(
        title=f"Sampling Distribution of theta_hat (n = {initial_n})",
        xaxis=dict(
            title="Estimated difference (theta_hat)",
            range=[bin_start, bin_end]
        ),
        yaxis=dict(title="Frequency"),
        shapes=[
            dict(
                type="line",
                x0=true_theta,
                x1=true_theta,
                y0=0,
                y1=1,
                yref="paper",
                line=dict(color="red", dash="dash", width=3)
            )
        ],
        sliders=[
            {
                "currentvalue": {"prefix": "Sample size: "},
                "pad": {"t": 40},
                "steps": [
                    {
                        "args": [
                            [str(n)],
                            {
                                "frame": {"duration": 0, "redraw": True},
                                "mode": "immediate",
                                "transition": {"duration": 0}
                            }
                        ],
                        "label": str(n),
                        "method": "animate"
                    }
                    for n in sample_sizes
                ]
            }
        ]
    ),
    frames=frames
)

fig.show()


                            
                                            








Note



Each individual observation is still either 0 or 1 (sign up or not). However, once we take multiple observations, we are no longer working with just 0s and 1s—we are working with averages.
For example, when \(n = 1\), a single draw might look like:
A: [1] → 1 B: [0] → 0
So the difference is: \[
\hat{\theta} = 1 - 0 = 1
\] In this case, there are only a few possible values, which is why the distribution looks very discrete.
Now, when \(n = 5\), we might observe something like:
A: [1, 0, 1, 0, 0] → 2/5 = 0.4 B: [0, 0, 1, 0, 0] → 1/5 = 0.2
The difference is now: \[
\hat{\theta} = 0.4 - 0.2 = 0.2
\]
Because we are averaging more observations, the estimator \(\hat{\theta} = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\) can take on many more possible values. As \(n\) increases, these values become more closely spaced, which is why the distribution starts to look smoother and less discrete.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing
Now that we have seen the CLT in action — that the sampling distribution of \(\hat\theta\) is approximately Normal for large samples — we can use this result to perform a formal hypothesis test.






Important



A hypothesis test is a method for using sample data to evaluate whether a claim about a population is likely to be true.


In this case, we start with a null hypothesis \(H_0: \theta = 0\), which means that the two CTAs produce the same sign-up rate and there is no real difference between them. The alternative hypothesis \(H_1: \theta \neq 0\) represents the idea that there is a difference between the two CTAs. The goal of the test is to use our sample data to determine whether there is enough evidence to reject the null hypothesis and conclude that a meaningful difference exists.
We standardize \(\hat{\theta}\) to understand how unusual our estimate is under the null hypothesis. Since \(H_0\) assumes the true difference is 0, we subtract 0 and divide by the standard error to measure how many standard deviations our estimate is from 0: \[
z = \frac{\hat\theta - 0}{SE(\hat\theta)}
\]
By the Central Limit Theorem, \(\hat{\theta}\) is approximately normally distributed for large samples, and under \(H_0\) it is centered at 0. Dividing by the standard error puts it on a standardized scale, so under the null we have \(z \dot\sim N(0,1)\).
Once we know this distribution, we can compute a p-value, which tells us how likely it is to observe a result this extreme if the null hypothesis were true.


Show slider code
import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from scipy.stats import norm

# Grid for standard normal curve
x = np.linspace(-4, 4, 1000)
y = norm.pdf(x)

# z-values the slider will move through
z_values = np.round(np.arange(0.0, 3.1, 0.1), 1)

frames = []

for z in z_values:
    right_mask = x >= z
    left_mask = x <= -z
    p_value = 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(z))

    frames.append(
        go.Frame(
            name=str(z),
            data=[
                go.Scatter(
                    x=x, y=y,
                    mode="lines",
                    name="Standard Normal",
                    line=dict(color="black")
                ),
                go.Scatter(
                    x=x[right_mask], y=y[right_mask],
                    mode="lines",
                    fill="tozeroy",
                    name="Right tail",
                    line=dict(color="red")
                ),
                go.Scatter(
                    x=x[left_mask], y=y[left_mask],
                    mode="lines",
                    fill="tozeroy",
                    name="Left tail",
                    line=dict(color="red")
                )
            ],
            layout=go.Layout(
                title=f"Standard Normal Distribution with z = {z:.1f} and p-value = {p_value:.4f}",
                shapes=[
                    dict(
                        type="line",
                        x0=z, x1=z, y0=0, y1=norm.pdf(z),
                        line=dict(color="blue", dash="dash", width=2)
                    ),
                    dict(
                        type="line",
                        x0=-z, x1=-z, y0=0, y1=norm.pdf(-z),
                        line=dict(color="blue", dash="dash", width=2)
                    )
                ]
            )
        )
    )

# Initial value
z0 = z_values[0]
right_mask = x >= z0
left_mask = x <= -z0
p0 = 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(z0))

fig = go.Figure(
    data=[
        go.Scatter(
            x=x, y=y,
            mode="lines",
            name="Standard Normal",
            line=dict(color="black")
        ),
        go.Scatter(
            x=x[right_mask], y=y[right_mask],
            mode="lines",
            fill="tozeroy",
            name="Right tail",
            line=dict(color="red")
        ),
        go.Scatter(
            x=x[left_mask], y=y[left_mask],
            mode="lines",
            fill="tozeroy",
            name="Left tail",
            line=dict(color="red")
        )
    ],
    layout=go.Layout(
        title=f"Standard Normal Distribution with z = {z0:.1f} and p-value = {p0:.4f}",
        xaxis=dict(title="z", range=[-4, 4]),
        yaxis=dict(title="Density"),
        shapes=[
            dict(
                type="line",
                x0=z0, x1=z0, y0=0, y1=norm.pdf(z0),
                line=dict(color="blue", dash="dash", width=2)
            ),
            dict(
                type="line",
                x0=-z0, x1=-z0, y0=0, y1=norm.pdf(-z0),
                line=dict(color="blue", dash="dash", width=2)
            )
        ],
        sliders=[
            {
                "currentvalue": {"prefix": "z-value: "},
                "pad": {"t": 40},
                "steps": [
                    {
                        "args": [
                            [str(z)],
                            {"frame": {"duration": 0, "redraw": True},
                             "mode": "immediate",
                             "transition": {"duration": 0}}
                        ],
                        "label": str(z),
                        "method": "animate"
                    }
                    for z in z_values
                ]
            }
        ]
    ),
    frames=frames
)

fig.show()


                            
                                            


The plot above shows the standard normal distribution under the null hypothesis. The dashed vertical lines represent the value of the test statistic \(z\) and its negative. The shaded areas in the tails represent the probability of observing a value at least this extreme if the null hypothesis were true. This shaded area is the p-value.
As the value of \(z\) moves farther away from 0, the shaded area gets smaller. This means the p-value decreases, which provides stronger evidence against the null hypothesis. In other words, large values of \(|z|\) correspond to results that would be unlikely to occur by chance if \(H_0\) were true.
In practice, we compare the p-value to a chosen significance level (often denoted \(\alpha\)) to decide whether to reject the null hypothesis. Common thresholds are:









Significance Level (\(\alpha\))
Confidence Level
Approx. Critical \(|z|\)
Interpretation




0.10
90%
1.64
Weak evidence against \(H_0\)


0.05
95%
1.96
Moderate evidence against \(H_0\)


0.01
99%
2.58
Strong evidence against \(H_0\)



If the p-value is less than \(\alpha\), we reject the null hypothesis. Otherwise, we do not reject it.
Another way to think about this is through confidence intervals. For example, a 95% confidence interval corresponds to a significance level of \(\alpha = 0.05\). If the interval does not include 0, that is equivalent to rejecting the null hypothesis at the 5% level.
This is sometimes referred to as a “t-test,” but that is not exactly what we are doing here. The classical t-test comes from a setting where the data are normally distributed and the variance is unknown, so we estimate it from the data. In that case, the resulting test statistic follows an exact t-distribution.
In our setting, however, the data are Bernoulli (0 or 1), not normally distributed, so there is no exact t-distribution result. Instead, we rely on the Central Limit Theorem, which tells us that our estimator is approximately normally distributed when the sample size is large. Because of this, our test statistic is better thought of as following an approximate standard normal distribution.
In practice, this distinction does not matter much for large samples, since the t-distribution and the standard normal distribution are very similar. However, it is still important to understand where each result comes from and why we are using this approximation.

LEFT OFF HERE!!!!

Using your simulated data, compute the test statistic and its associated p-value (using the standard Normal distribution as the reference). Interpret the result for the reader. :::

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The T-Test as a Regression






Note




It turns out that the two-sample test we just ran is mathematically equivalent to a simple linear regression. Understanding this connection is useful because it means the same machinery — and the same software — that you use for regression can also perform hypothesis tests, and it generalizes naturally to more complex experimental designs.
Explain the setup to the reader. Stack the CTA A and CTA B data into a single dataset with two columns: an outcome \(Y_i\) (1 if the visitor signed up, 0 otherwise) and an indicator \(D_i\) (1 if the visitor saw CTA A, 0 if they saw CTA B). Then fit the regression:

\[
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 D_i + \varepsilon_i
\]

Walk through the interpretation of the coefficients:

\(\beta_0\) is the expected value of \(Y_i\) when \(D_i = 0\) — i.e., the mean of the B group, which estimates \(\pi_B\).
\(\beta_0 + \beta_1\) is the expected value of \(Y_i\) when \(D_i = 1\) — i.e., the mean of the A group, which estimates \(\pi_A\).
Therefore \(\beta_1 = \pi_A - \pi_B = \theta\), and the OLS estimate \(\hat\beta_1\) is numerically identical to \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\).

Fit this regression using your simulated data. Report the coefficient estimate \(\hat\beta_1\), its standard error, its t-statistic, and its p-value. Compare these to the values you computed in the previous section using the two-sample approach — they should match (up to a small difference in how the standard error is computed; standard OLS assumes a single pooled variance, while the two-sample formula we used assumes separate variances for each group).
Explain to the reader why this equivalence matters: the regression framework makes it easy to add controls, interactions, or additional treatment arms, all while producing the same inference in the simple case.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Problem with Peeking






Note




Your boss is impatient. Rather than waiting until all 1,000 visitors have been assigned to each group, she wants to run the hypothesis test after every 100 visitors per group — peeking at the results after 100, 200, 300, …, up to 1,000 visitors per group (10 tests in total). If any of those tests comes back significant at the \(\alpha = 0.05\) level, she wants to declare a winner and stop the experiment early.
Explain to the reader why this is problematic under classical frequentist statistics. The key issue is that a single test at \(\alpha = 0.05\) has a 5% chance of a false positive (rejecting \(H_0\) when it is actually true). But when you conduct multiple tests on accumulating data, each peek is another opportunity to get a false positive. The overall probability of at least one false rejection across all the peeks is much higher than 5%.
Demonstrate this with a simulation. The idea is as follows:

Set up a world where the null hypothesis is true: \(\pi_A = \pi_B = 0.20\) (i.e., \(\theta = 0\), there is no difference between the CTAs).
Simulate a single experiment: draw 1,000 observations for group A and 1,000 for group B. After every 100 observations per group (at \(n = 100, 200, \ldots, 1000\)), compute the z-statistic and p-value. Record whether any of the 10 p-values falls below 0.05.
Repeat step 2 a total of 10,000 times to simulate 10,000 experiments.
Compute the fraction of those 10,000 experiments in which at least one peek produced a significant result. This is the empirical false positive rate under peeking.

Compare this to the nominal 5% false positive rate of a single test. Report the result and explain to the reader why peeking inflates the Type I error rate and what this means in practice for running A/B tests.
